stem: For the series \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n \) with \( a_n = \dfrac{[[a]]n + ([[b]])}{[[c]]n + ([[d]])} \), the nth-term (divergence) test examines \( \lim_{n\to\infty} a_n \). What is that limit?
solution: Numerator and denominator both have degree \( 1 \) in \( n \), so \( \lim_{n\to\infty} a_n = \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[c]]} = [[answer]] \). Since this is nonzero, the divergence test shows \( \sum a_n \) diverges.
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio of the leading (degree-1) coefficients.
This takes the ratio of the constant terms, which do not determine the limit.
The degrees are equal, so the limit is the coefficient ratio, not \( 0 \); a \( 0 \) limit would make the test inconclusive.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by two from the correct limit.

stem: For the series \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n \) with \( a_n = \dfrac{[[a]]n + ([[b]])}{[[c]]n^{2} + ([[e]])} \), what is \( \lim_{n\to\infty} a_n \)?
solution: The numerator has degree \( 1 \) but the denominator has degree \( 2 \), so \( \lim_{n\to\infty} a_n = 0 \). Since the limit is \( 0 \), the nth-term test is INCONCLUSIVE here (it cannot show divergence, and does not by itself show convergence either).
distractor_rationales: This is the ratio of leading coefficients, which only determines the limit when the degrees are EQUAL; here the denominator's degree is larger.
This keeps only the numerator's leading coefficient, ignoring that the denominator grows faster.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by two from the correct limit.
This is off by two from the correct limit.

stem: For the series \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n \) with \( a_n = \left(1 + \dfrac{[[c]]}{n}\right)^{n} \), the nth-term test examines \( \lim_{n\to\infty} a_n \). What is that limit?
solution: This is the standard compound-growth limit \( \lim_{n\to\infty}\left(1+\frac{c}{n}\right)^{n} = e^{c} \), so the limit is \( e^{[[c]]} = [[answer]] \). Since \( c \ne 0 \) this is nonzero, so by the nth-term test \( \sum a_n \) diverges.
distractor_rationales: This is the limit one would get if \( c \) were \( 0 \); it ignores the actual value of \( c \).
This uses \( -c \) in the exponent instead of \( c \).
This mistakes the parameter \( c \) itself for the limit of the whole expression.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This doubles the correct limit.

stem: For the series \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n \) with \( a_n = [[c]] + \dfrac{\cos([[d]]n)}{n} \), the nth-term test examines \( \lim_{n\to\infty} a_n \). Using the fact that \( \cos([[d]]n) \) is bounded, what is that limit?
solution: Since \( -1 \le \cos([[d]]n) \le 1 \), the term \( \dfrac{\cos([[d]]n)}{n} \to 0 \) by the squeeze theorem, so \( \lim_{n\to\infty} a_n = [[c]] = [[answer]] \). This is nonzero, so \( \sum a_n \) diverges by the nth-term test.
distractor_rationales: This assumes the whole expression vanishes, but the bounded oscillating piece only removes the \( \cos \) term, leaving \( c \).
This adds an extra \( 1 \) from the bound on cosine instead of letting the oscillating term vanish.
This subtracts an extra \( 1 \) from the bound on cosine instead of letting the oscillating term vanish.
This is off by two from the correct limit.
This is off by two from the correct limit.
This confuses the frequency parameter \( d \) inside the cosine with the limit itself.

stem: For the series \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n \) with \( a_n = \sqrt[n]{[[a]]n + [[b]]} \), the nth-term test examines \( \lim_{n\to\infty} a_n \). What is that limit?
solution: Any polynomial in \( n \) taken to the \( 1/n \) power tends to \( 1 \): \( \lim_{n\to\infty} \sqrt[n]{[[a]]n+[[b]]} = 1 = [[answer]] \). Since this limit is nonzero, the nth-term test shows \( \sum a_n \) diverges.
distractor_rationales: This wrongly assumes an \( n \)th root of a growing quantity shrinks to \( 0 \).
This mistakes the linear growth rate \( a \) for the limit of the \( n \)th root.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by two from the correct limit.
This is off by two from the correct limit.
